title: Preparation
content:
1. Prerequisites Before starting, ensure you have the following installed:
Visual Studio 2022 or VS Code. .NET 8 SDK. Node.js (v18+) and npm. AWS CLI (configured via aws configure). Postman for API testing. 2. Get the Source Code Clone the project repository:
git clone https://github.com/hoanganh-p/url-shortener-backend.git git clone https://github.com/hoanganh-p/url-shortener-frontend.git

title: Workshop
content:
Serverless URL Shortener Welcome to the Workshop. In this section, we will build a complete URL Shortener application using Serverless architecture on AWS.
The main goal is to get familiar with and combine core AWS services to solve real-world problems regarding cost optimization and scalability.
AWS Services Used This project will focus on the following services:
AWS Lambda: Serverless compute service running backend code (.NET 8) without managing servers. Amazon DynamoDB: High-performance NoSQL database for storing user and link information. Amazon API Gateway: RESTful API entry point, receiving requests from the Internet and forwarding them to Lambda. Amazon S3: Static web hosting for Frontend (ReactJS). Amazon CloudFront: CDN for global Frontend content delivery and API caching. AWS IAM: Managing secure access permissions between services. System Architecture Frontend: Users access the web interface hosted on S3 and distributed via CloudFront. API: Requests from the Frontend are sent to API Gateway. Backend: API Gateway triggers AWS Lambda to execute business logic. Database: Lambda interacts with DynamoDB to read/write data. Let’s get started!

title: Backend Deployment
content:
1. Create DynamoDB Table Table Name: Url. Partition Key: shortcode (String). 2. Deploy AWS Lambda Use dotnet lambda deploy-function to upload your .NET code.
Runtime: .NET 8 Handler: UrlShortener.Api 3. Configure API Gateway Create a new REST API.
Manually create Resources and Methods structure (integrate Lambda):
/{shortCode}: GET, OPTIONS /api/auth: /login: POST, OPTIONS /register: POST, OPTIONS /api/url`: /create: POST, OPTIONS /{code}: GET, DELETE, OPTIONS /api/user: /all: GET, OPTIONS Enable CORS (Important):
Select root Resource / -> Actions -> Enable CORS. Ensure the CORS policy allows the CloudFront domain (obtained in step 5.3). builder.Services.AddCors(options => { options.AddPolicy("AllowCors", policy => { policy.WithOrigins("https://d2wnt86s7rdbuv.cloudfront.net") .AllowAnyHeader() .AllowAnyMethod(); }); }); Deploy API to prod stage.

title: Frontend Deployment
content: 1. Create S3 Bucket Create a new bucket (e.g., urlshortener-frontend-bucket). Enable Static website hosting. 2. Configure CloudFront Create a Distribution pointing to S3 (Frontend) and API Gateway (Backend). Set Origin Path /prod for API Gateway origin. Set Redirect HTTP to HTTPS. 3. Build & Upload React App Update API_URL in React code to use CloudFront domain. const AWS_API_URL = 'https://d2s5r61an6mtu1.cloudfront.net'; Build the project: npm run build Upload the build folder content to S3 Bucket.

title: System Testing
content: 1. Access Website Go to your CloudFront domain (e.g., https://d2wnt86s7rdbuv.cloudfront.net/). Register a new account and Login. 2. Create Short Link Enter a long URL (e.g., Google Docs). Click Shorten. 3. Verify Redirect Click on the generated short link and verify if it redirects correctly.

title: Cleanup Resources
content:
Delete resources to avoid costs After finishing the workshop, delete resources in the following order:
S3 Bucket: Empty and delete the bucket. CloudFront: Disable and Delete distribution. API Gateway: Delete API. Lambda Function: Delete function. DynamoDB Table: Delete table.
